title: Polvorones
subtitle: Spanish Christmas shortbread that dissolves in the mouth
excerpt: Polvorones are made with toasted flour, which removes gluten development and gives an exceptionally short, crumbly texture that literally turns to dust on the tongue. Lard is traditional but butter works equally well. They are fragile, individually wrapped in paper and eaten at Christmas throughout Spain. The process takes about 45 minutes.
sourceNotes: Polvorones originate in Estepa, Andalusia, where commercial production began in the sixteenth century. The recipe is documented in Ruperto de Nola's 'Libro de Cozina' (1529). They became a national Christmas tradition across Spain and were exported to Latin America, where regional variants developed. The toasted-flour technique is the defining characteristic and appears consistently across historical and contemporary sources.

recipe.makeAheadNotes: Polvorones keep for 2 weeks at room temperature in an airtight tin. Wrap individually in squares of greaseproof paper to prevent them crumbling.

baking.bakeTemperatureNote: fan oven; bake until just set and very lightly coloured, about 12 to 15 minutes

glossaryTerms.definition: Heating plain flour in a dry frying pan or moderate oven, stirring constantly, until it turns a light biscuit colour and smells nutty. Toasting drives off moisture and partially breaks down the starch, reducing the flour's ability to absorb fat and develop gluten when mixed. In polvorones, this produces a dough so short it barely holds together, and a biscuit that dissolves almost instantly on the tongue. Stir constantly over medium heat and remove from the pan as soon as the colour appears, as it continues to cook in the residual heat.

content.text: The defining step in polvorones is

. Stir the flour in a dry pan over medium heat for about 8 minutes until it turns a warm biscuit colour and smells nutty. The moment the colour appears, tip it onto a plate to stop the cooking. Overtoasted flour tastes bitter.

Lard is traditional; butter gives a slightly less crumbly result but works well. The dough is fragile once mixed. Handle it as little as possible: press it out and cut shapes rather than rolling, then move them to the tray with a palette knife.

items.prepNote: toasted in a dry pan until lightly golden

content.text: Put the 250 g flour in a wide frying pan over medium heat. Stir constantly for 7 to 9 minutes until the flour has turned a uniform light biscuit colour and smells distinctly nutty. Tip onto a plate and leave to cool completely.

Heat the oven to 180 degrees C fan. Combine the cooled toasted flour, 30 g ground almonds, 100 g icing sugar and 1 tsp cinnamon. Add the 120 g softened butter in pieces and work in with your fingertips until the dough just comes together. It will be very short and crumbly. Press out to 1.5 cm thick on a lightly floured surface and cut into rounds or oval shapes with a cutter or glass. Use a palette knife to transfer to a lined baking tray.

Bake for 12 to 15 minutes until very lightly golden at the edges and set on top. They will look and feel soft. Leave on the tray until completely cold before moving, as they are extremely fragile when warm. Dust generously with icing sugar.

items.problem: Dough crumbles and will not hold together at all
items.solution: The flour may have been toasted too long, removing too much moisture and fat-absorbing capacity. Try adding the butter slightly warmer (more liquid), and press the dough very firmly together. If it still will not hold, add 1 teaspoon of milk.

items.problem: Polvorones broke when moved from the tray
items.solution: They were moved while still warm. Leave them on the tray until completely cold; they firm significantly as they cool and can then be lifted with a thin palette knife.

The flour was toasted too long or over too high a heat. The colour should be a uniform pale biscuit, not brown. Reduce the heat and stir more frequently, and tip onto a cold plate immediately when the colour is right.
